name: mobilenet

members.name: MobileNetV1Config
members.signature: MobileNetV1Config(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, width_mult: float = 1.0, dropout: float = 0.001)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, width_mult: float = 1.0, dropout: float = 0.001)

members.extended:
`width_mult` — uniform channel multiplier (α in the paper).
  1.0 → full model; 0.75 / 0.5 / 0.25 → slimmer variants.

`resolution_mult` — spatial resolution multiplier (ρ).
  Typically applied to the input, not the architecture itself.
  Kept here for documentation parity; not enforced inside the model.

`dropout` — classifier dropout (0.001 in the original paper).

members.name: MobileNetV1

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the ReLU module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

members.summary: MobileNet v1 feature-extracting backbone (no classification head).
members.extended:
Implements the depthwise-separable convolutional topology from
Howard et al., "MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural
Networks for Mobile Vision Applications", [arXiv:1704.04861](https://arxiv.org/abs/1704.04861), 2017.
The network alternates a per-channel $3 \times 3$ depthwise
convolution with a $1 \times 1$ pointwise convolution that
mixes channels — a factorisation that reduces FLOPs by roughly
$1/N + 1/D_K^2$ relative to a standard
$D_K \times D_K$ convolution, an 8–9× saving for the
$3 \times 3$ case.

The body is a 3×3 stem (stride 2) followed by 13 depthwise +
pointwise blocks producing feature maps at strides 2, 4, 8, 16,
and 32 relative to the input.  A global average pool collapses
the final spatial map to a single 1×1 descriptor.  This class
is the canonical feature extractor used by detection /
segmentation heads when deployed on mobile / embedded devices.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use the factory functions
(`mobilenet_v1`, `mobilenet_v1_075`, …) for
paper-cited width-multiplier variants.

examples:
Build a MobileNet-v1 backbone and run a forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet import mobilenet_v1
>>> backbone = mobilenet_v1()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(2, 1024, 1, 1)
Inspect the per-stage feature map descriptors for FPN integration:
>>> info = backbone.feature_info
>>> [(fi.stage, fi.num_channels, fi.reduction) for fi in info]
[(1, 64, 2), (2, 128, 4), (3, 256, 8), (4, 512, 16), (5, 1024, 32)]
notes:
Each depthwise-separable block computes

$$
\mathrm{PW}\big(\sigma(\mathrm{BN}(\mathrm{DW}_{3\times3}(x)))\big)
\;\;\to\;\; \sigma(\mathrm{BN}(\,\cdot\,)),
$$
where $\mathrm{DW}_{3\times3}$ is the per-channel
$3 \times 3$ spatial filter (`groups=in_channels`),
$\mathrm{PW}$ is the $1 \times 1$ channel-mixing
convolution, and $\sigma$ is ReLU.  The width multiplier
$\alpha \in (0, 1]$ uniformly scales every channel count
so that the same architecture can target sub-mW edge devices
($\alpha = 0.25$) up to full desktop deployment
($\alpha = 1.0$).

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: features
attributes.description:
Stem $3 \times 3$ conv (stride 2) followed by 13
depthwise+pointwise blocks; channel counts scale with
`config.width_mult`.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Global average pool collapsing the final feature map to
`(B, C, 1, 1)`.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Per-stage descriptor (channels + reduction factor) exposed
via `BackboneMixin` for downstream FPN / decoder
modules.

members.name: MobileNetV1ForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: MobileNet v1 with global-average-pooled linear classification head.
members.extended:
Combines a `MobileNetV1` backbone with the standard
ImageNet classification head: an `lucid.nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d`
to pool every spatial location into a single feature vector,
a `lucid.nn.Dropout` (probability `config.dropout`,
default 0.001 per the paper), and a `lucid.nn.Linear`
projection to `config.num_classes` logits.  When `labels`
are supplied to `forward`, a cross-entropy loss is
computed and returned alongside the logits.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use the `*_cls` factory functions
(`mobilenet_v1_cls`, `mobilenet_v1_075_cls`, …)
to obtain a paper-cited configuration; pass a custom config
to retarget `num_classes` or change `width_mult`.

examples:
Run inference on a batch of 224×224 RGB images:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet import mobilenet_v1_cls
>>> model = mobilenet_v1_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(4, 1000)
>>> out.loss is None
True
Compute a loss given labels (e.g. during training):
>>> labels = lucid.tensor([0, 1, 2, 3], dtype=lucid.int64)
>>> out = model(x, labels=labels)
>>> out.loss.shape
()
notes:
The classification flow is

$$
\text{logits} = W \,\operatorname{Drop}\!\left(
    \operatorname{GAP}(\,\mathrm{backbone}(x)\,)
\right) + b,
$$
where $\operatorname{GAP}$ is the global average pool.
Loss is the standard categorical cross-entropy, computed only
when `labels` is not `None`.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Global average pool collapsing the final feature map to
`1 × 1`.

attributes.name: drop
attributes.description:
Dropout layer (probability `config.dropout`) applied
before the linear classifier.

members.name: mobilenet_v1
members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV1` with the canonical paper topology:
a 3×3 stem (stride 2) followed by 13 depthwise+pointwise blocks,
yielding approximately 4.2M parameters.  Howard et al., 2017
report a 70.6% ImageNet-1k top-1 validation accuracy with this
configuration (Table 7).  The default choice when the full
accuracy budget is available.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored — the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `MobileNetV1Config`
(e.g. `in_channels=1` for grayscale input).

returns.description:
Backbone with the MobileNet-v1 ($\alpha = 1.0$)
configuration applied (or with `overrides` merged on top
of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet import mobilenet_v1
>>> model = mobilenet_v1()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 1024, 1, 1)
notes:
See Howard et al., "MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural
Networks for Mobile Vision Applications", [arXiv:1704.04861](https://arxiv.org/abs/1704.04861), 2017,
Table 1 and Table 7.

members.name: mobilenet_v1_cls
members.summary: MobileNet-v1 image classifier at width multiplier $\alpha = 1.0$.
members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV1ForImageClassification` with the
canonical paper topology (13 depthwise+pointwise blocks) followed
by global average pooling and a linear projection to
`config.num_classes` (default 1000 for ImageNet-1k).
Approximately 4.2M parameters and 70.6% ImageNet-1k top-1 in
Howard et al., 2017 (Table 7).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `MobileNetV1Config`
(typically `num_classes` to retarget the classifier).

returns.description:
Classifier with the MobileNet-v1 ($\alpha = 1.0$)
configuration applied (or with `overrides` merged on top
of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet import mobilenet_v1_cls
>>> model = mobilenet_v1_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 10)
notes:
See Howard et al., "MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural
Networks for Mobile Vision Applications", [arXiv:1704.04861](https://arxiv.org/abs/1704.04861), 2017,
Table 7.

members.name: mobilenet_v1_075
members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV1` with every channel count multiplied
by 0.75 — approximately 2.6M parameters.  Howard et al., 2017
report 68.4% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy with this configuration
(Table 7), at roughly 60% of the FLOPs of the full-width model.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

returns.description:
Backbone with the MobileNet-v1 ($\alpha = 0.75$)
configuration applied (or with `overrides` merged on top
of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.mobilenet import mobilenet_v1_075
>>> model = mobilenet_v1_075()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 768, 1, 1)
notes:
See Howard et al., "MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural
Networks for Mobile Vision Applications", [arXiv:1704.04861](https://arxiv.org/abs/1704.04861), 2017,
Table 7.

members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV1` with every channel count multiplied
by 0.5 — approximately 1.3M parameters.  Howard et al., 2017
report 63.7% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy with this configuration
(Table 7), at roughly 27% of the FLOPs of the full-width model.

members.name: mobilenet_v1_025
members.extended:
Builds a `MobileNetV1` with every channel count multiplied
by 0.25 — approximately 0.5M parameters.  Howard et al., 2017
report 50.6% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy with this configuration
(Table 7) — the smallest MobileNet-v1 variant, targeted at
extreme edge deployments where parameter count is the binding
constraint.

>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)

>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 256, 1, 1)
notes:
See Howard et al., "MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural
Networks for Mobile Vision Applications", [arXiv:1704.04861](https://arxiv.org/abs/1704.04861), 2017,
Table 7.

summary: MobileNet v1 family — Howard et al., 2017.
citation: Howard, Andrew G., et al. "MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural Networks for Mobile Vision Applications." arXiv preprint [arXiv:1704.04861](https://arxiv.org/abs/1704.04861), 2017.
theory:
MobileNet is built around the *depthwise separable convolution*, a
factorisation that decomposes a standard convolution into two much
cheaper stages: a per-channel **depthwise** $3 \times 3$
spatial filter followed by a $1 \times 1$ **pointwise**
convolution that mixes channels.  For an input of $M$
channels and an output of $N$ channels with kernel size
$D_K$, the cost drops from
$D_K \cdot D_K \cdot M \cdot N \cdot D_F \cdot D_F$ to



$$
D_K \cdot D_K \cdot M \cdot D_F \cdot D_F
\;+\; M \cdot N \cdot D_F \cdot D_F,
$$

a reduction of roughly $1/N + 1/D_K^2$.  For
$D_K = 3$ and the typical $N$ of a few hundred,
that is an 8–9× FLOPs saving with only a small accuracy loss
on ImageNet.

Two scalar hyper-parameters expose a smooth accuracy–latency
trade-off curve at inference time without retraining the whole
family.  The **width multiplier** $\alpha \in (0, 1]$
uniformly thins every layer (input and output channels become
$\alpha M$ and $\alpha N$), giving four standard
variants at $\alpha \in \{1.0, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25\}$.  The
**resolution multiplier** $\rho$ rescales the input spatial
size — it does not change the model itself, only the feature
maps flowing through it, and is therefore not enforced inside
the network.

Together these two knobs let MobileNet-v1 cover a wide spectrum
of mobile/embedded budgets from sub-mW edge devices up to
desktop-class deployment, while keeping a single training recipe
and a single :class:`MobileNetV1Config` schema.
